claims.claim: We investigate whether a lightweight online Attention Budget Controller (ABC) can improve fixed-budget token retention in long-context inference by dynamically splitting a constrained KV-cache budget between a recency rail (for local, recently streamed tokens) and a utility rail (for salient, repeatedly attended long-range facts).

evidence_refs.quote: # Attention Budget Controller: Adaptive Recency–Utility Split for Fixed-Budget KV Retention

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: ABC achieves the highest hit rate on nonstationary mixed-shift workloads at budgets 64, 128, and 512, and is within 0.003 of the best policy at budget 256, with improvements over LRU ranging from +0.116 to +0.434 absolute hit rate.

evidence_refs.quote: # Attention Budget Controller: Adaptive Recency–Utility Split for Fixed-Budget KV Retention

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: However, ABC is not a universal replacement for specialized policies: on pure recency workloads, LRU remains superior (ABC underperforms by 0.004–0.078); on pure landmark workloads, heavy-hitter retains a small edge at tight budgets (ABC underperforms by 0.006–0.081).

claims.claim: Long-context transformer inference faces a fundamental memory constraint: the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, and at context lengths of 128k tokens or more, retaining all key-value states becomes infeasible.
